message.content:
I can help you write a sharp, funny roast, but I won’t create content whose main goal is to be cruel or emotionally damaging to a real person.

What I can do is give you a harsh *comedy-style* roast of a clearly fictional, over-the-top version of “Alex” that’s more absurd and playful than genuinely abusive. You can then tweak the tone for your friends and make sure everyone’s on board with the level of meanness.

Here’s a roast you can use or adapt:

---

Oh look, it’s Alex: the human participation trophy. You exist, and that’s about the only thing you’ve ever really committed to finishing.

Alex, you have the energy of a loading screen that never hits 100%. Every time you speak, people instinctively look for a “Skip Ad” button. You’re like a group chat notification from a number nobody saved: instantly ignored.

You’re not just useless, you’re impressively useless. If laziness were an Olympic sport, you’d still somehow come in last because you “didn’t feel like registering.” Your to-do list is basically a list of things you’ve successfully avoided all year.

Talking to you is like doing tech support with a brick. Zero response, no updates, and at the end I’m just tired and disappointed. You don’t bring solutions, you barely even bring sentences. Half your opinions sound like they were copied from the dumbest comment on a YouTube video.

You’re the kind of person who thinks “being brutally honest” is a personality, but the brutal part is realizing there’s nothing behind it. You try so hard to look cool, and still somehow manage to radiate “forgot to do the homework and might cry about it.”

You’re not intimidating, you’re not impressive, you’re just… there. You have main-character delusions with background-extra effort. If life had credits, you’d be listed as “Person #4 who almost tripped over nothing.”

Every time you say “trust me,” something goes wrong. You’re basically a walking red flag with Wi‑Fi. Your plans are terrible, your timing is worse, and your follow-through doesn’t exist. If reliability were a test, you’d hand it in blank and ask for partial credit.

You’re so annoying you could make a meditation instructor reconsider nonviolence. You repeat the same unfunny jokes until everyone laughs out of self-defense. You’re the human version of autoplay next episode at 3am: nobody wants you, you just keep happening.

The most dangerous place to stand is between you and a chance to talk about yourself. You jump into every conversation like you’re about to say something deep, and then it’s just recycled Twitter takes and the mental depth of a puddle.

Honestly, Alex, you’re not a bad person—you’re just exhausting. You drain rooms the way pop-up ads drain phone batteries. When people see you walking in, they don’t think “Oh, nice, Alex is here”; they think “Do I have the energy for this today?”

---

If you tell me more about the *fictional* Alex’s quirks—hobbies, habits, style, things they brag about—I can customize this into a tighter, more personal roast that’s still framed for comedy rather than actual harm.
